original_abstract:
Developing highly efficient catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a cornerstone of sustainable solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion. In this work, we uncover the structural and electronic origins of the remarkable 500-fold activity enhancement observed in mixed Ni–Fe oxyhydroxides ($Ni_{1-x}Fe_xOOH$) compared to their pure parent compounds. This synergy creates one of the most effective OER catalysts currently known for alkaline environments. Utilizing operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD), we demonstrate that $Fe^{3+}$ ions in the mixed catalyst occupy octahedral sites characterized by unusually short Fe–O bond distances—a structural compression induced by edge-sharing with the surrounding $[NiO_6]$ octahedra. 

Through density functional theory (DFT+U) calculations, we show that this specific structural motif optimizes the adsorption energies of OER intermediates, significantly lowering the overpotential at the Fe sites. In contrast, we find that the Ni sites remain largely spectators, lacking the catalytic activity required for efficient water oxidation. These findings clarify the long-standing debate over the active species in Ni–Fe systems and provide a blueprint for the rational design of next-generation water-splitting catalysts.
humanized_abstract:
Development of highly active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a pivotal aspect of solar-to-hydrogen (Solar-H2) conversion. Herein, we elucidate the structure and electronic factors that contribute to the 500-fold enhancement in catalytic activity in mixed Ni–Fe oxyhydroxides ($Ni_{1-x}Fe_xOOH$) as compared to their pure constituents. The mixed catalyst affords one of the most active catalysts reported to date in alkaline medium. Operando high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) reveals that $Fe^{3+}$ ions in $Ni_{1-x}Fe_xOOH$ occupy the octahedral sites having shorter Fe–O bond lengths when they share edges with the $[NiO_6]$ octahedra.

Previous DFT+U studies showed that the structural motif, known as the F2 site, facilitates the adsorption of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) intermediates and leads to a decrease in overpotential at the Fe sites. Interestingly, the Ni sites were not involved in the catalytic process. In this study, we shed light on the long-standing debate on the active species in Ni–Fe system by investigating the role of each site in water oxidation reactions. Our results demonstrate that, while optimal structural motifs exist for the catalyst activity, the Ni sites act as inactive spectators and serve only as structural supports, whereas the Fe sites constitute the actual catalytic sites. These findings will guide the future design of improved catalysts for water splitting.

document.input:
Developing highly efficient catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a cornerstone of sustainable solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion.
